name: The Shape of Salah
title: The Shape of Salah: How the Prayer Looks, for Beginners
meta_description: Before the words, learn the form. A gentle guide to the physical shape of the Muslim prayer for new Muslims: the five prayers, the rak'ah, and the postures of standing, bowing, and prostration.
keywords: how to pray step by step
lede: You have washed. Now you stand. Salah, the formal prayer, is the second pillar of Islam and the thing you will do most often as a Muslim, five times every day. It is shorter than you fear, only a few minutes each time, and once your body knows the shape, it carries you.

Today we learn only the form: where the body goes, how you stand and bow and lower yourself to the ground. The words come tomorrow. We are building the prayer in two gentle halves, the frame first, then the voice. Let us raise the frame.

do_today.label: Just for today
do_today.text: In a private space, try the movements once, without any words yet: stand still, then bend at the waist into a bow, then lower yourself until your forehead rests on the floor. Stay there for one breath. That posture, your face on the ground, is the heart of the prayer. You just touched it.

sections.h2: Five times a day, and why
paras: The prayer is not a heavy ritual dropped on you. It is a rhythm, a returning. Five short times a day you stop whatever you are doing and remember the One who made you, so that the whole day stays tied to Him and never fully drifts. Allah gave the reason in the simplest words:

ayah.arabic: إِنَّنِىٓ أَنَا ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنَا۠ فَٱعْبُدْنِى وَأَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ لِذِكْرِىٓ
ayah.text: Indeed, I am Allah. There is no deity except Me, so worship Me and establish prayer for My remembrance.

paras: The five prayers have names and rough times: Fajr before sunrise, Dhuhr after midday, Asr in the afternoon, Maghrib just after sunset, and Isha at night. You do not have to memorize the exact timings today; a prayer-times app or a local mosque will tell you when each one is for your city.
That these times are fixed is a mercy, not a chain. They are appointments with peace, spaced through your day. The Qur'an calls them exactly that:

ayah.arabic: إِنَّ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ كَانَتْ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ كِتَٰبًا مَّوْقُوتًا
ayah.text: Indeed, prayer has been decreed upon the believers a decree of specified times.

paras: Many people pray only in their heads. Islam prays with the whole body. You will stand, you will bow, you will kneel and place your forehead on the ground. The body acts out what the heart is saying, so that humility is not just a thought but something you can feel in your knees and your spine. Allah pairs the prayer with the bowing itself:

ayah.arabic: وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَٱرْكَعُوا۟ مَعَ ٱلرَّٰكِعِينَ
ayah.text: And establish prayer and give zakah and bow with those who bow.

paras: Prayer is built from a repeating unit called a rak'ah. One rak'ah is a single cycle of standing, bowing, and prostrating. The five daily prayers are simply different numbers of these cycles, two here, three or four there, but once you know one rak'ah, you know them all. So let us learn one.

You begin standing, facing the qiblah, which is the direction of the Kaaba, the first house built for the worship of Allah, in the city of Makkah. A compass or a phone app will point the way; if you are ever unsure, do your best, and Allah accepts that.
You raise your hands and say 'Allahu akbar,' Allah is the greatest. This is the takbir, and it opens the prayer. From this moment, the world is set aside. It is just you and your Lord.
You stand quietly, hands folded over your body, and you recite (the words come in tomorrow's lesson).
Then you bow: you bend at the waist, back flat, hands resting on your knees, and you glorify your Lord. This bowing is called ruku.
You rise to stand straight again, praising Him.

Then you go down to the ground in prostration: your forehead and nose, both palms, both knees, and the toes of both feet all touching the earth. This is sujud, and the Prophet ﷺ taught that it is the closest a servant ever comes to Allah. Your highest part, your face, is laid on the lowest place, the floor. Pride has nowhere to stand here.

You sit up for a moment, then prostrate a second time. That completes one rak'ah. Then you stand and begin the next, or, at the end of the prayer, you sit for the closing words and turn your face to the right and the left to finish.

sections.h2: Pray as you have seen me pray
paras: You do not have to design any of this. The Prophet ﷺ prayed in front of his Companions for years, and they passed every movement down to us. He said simply:

hadith.text: Pray as you have seen me praying.

paras: The easiest way to learn the prayer is to see it. If you can get to a mosque, stand in a row and follow the people around you; the body learns faster than the book. The schools of law differ on small things, where exactly the hands rest, when they are raised, and all of those views are valid and beloved. Do not let the small differences frighten you. Learn the shape, pray it, and let a local teacher polish the details over time.

And know what you are stepping into. The prayer is not a tax you pay to God. It is, five times a day, a door back to peace. When you place your forehead on the ground, you set down the weight you have been carrying since the last time you prayed. The One you stand before is Himself the source of that peace, and one of His names, As-Salam, means exactly that.

dua.arabic: رَبِّ ٱجْعَلْنِى مُقِيمَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِى ۚ رَبَّنَا وَتَقَبَّلْ دُعَآءِ
dua.translit: Rabbi-j'alni muqima-s-salati wa min dhurriyyati, Rabbana wa taqabbal du'a
dua.meaning: My Lord, make me an establisher of prayer, and many from my descendants. Our Lord, and accept my supplication. (Ibrahim 14:40)

lessons_intro: If you remember nothing else from this page, remember these four things about the prayer's shape.

lessons.bold: Prayer is built from one repeating unit.
lessons.text: It is called a rak'ah: stand, bow, prostrate. Learn one, and you know them all.

lessons.bold: The whole body worships, not just the mind.
lessons.text: You stand, you bow, you lay your forehead on the ground. Humility you can feel.

lessons.bold: Sujud is the closest you come to Allah.
lessons.text: Your face, your highest part, on the floor. Pride has nowhere to stand.
'Pray as you have seen me pray.' A row in the mosque teaches faster than any page.

faq.a: Salah is the formal Muslim prayer, performed five times a day. It is the second pillar of Islam and joins words with physical postures, standing, bowing, and prostrating, in a short, repeating cycle. Each prayer takes only a few minutes.

faq.q: What are the five daily prayers?
faq.a: Fajr before sunrise, Dhuhr after midday, Asr in the afternoon, Maghrib just after sunset, and Isha at night. A prayer-times app or a local mosque will give the exact times for your city. You do not have to memorize them all today.
A rak'ah is one full cycle of the prayer: standing and reciting, then bowing, then prostrating twice. The five prayers are made of different numbers of rak'ahs, but the unit is always the same.

Sujud is the prostration: placing the forehead, nose, palms, knees, and toes on the ground. The Prophet ﷺ taught that it is the moment a servant is closest to Allah, and it is the heart of the prayer.

faq.q: What if I cannot stand or bow because of illness or injury?
faq.a: Then you pray as you are able: sitting, or even lying down, with small movements of the head. Allah does not burden a soul beyond its capacity. Pray in whatever way your body allows, and it is fully accepted.

closing_heading: A du'a as you learn to stand before Him
closing: Today your body learned the shape of nearness: to stand, to bow, to lay your face on the ground before the One you testified to. It may feel strange the first few times. With a little practice it becomes the steadiest part of your day.

Tomorrow we give the prayer its voice. You will learn the words that go with these postures, beginning with the opening of the Qur'an, and you will build one complete rak'ah from start to finish. The frame is up. Tomorrow we fill it with light.

O Allah, make me one who stands before You, who bows and is not too proud, who lays his forehead on the ground and finds peace there. Make the prayer the rest of my day, and not its burden, and accept it from me, weak as it begins. Ameen.
source_note: Qur'an citations (20:14, 4:103, 2:43, and the du'a from 14:40) are from the Saheeh International translation, verified against the canonical Arabic text via quran.ai (Arabic in Uthmani script, edition ar-uthmani-minimal). Hadith: 'Pray as you have seen me praying,' Sahih al-Bukhari 631 (sahih); the prostration as the closest a servant comes to his Lord, Sahih Muslim 482 (sahih). FOR SCHOLAR REVIEW: this lesson describes the physical form of salah. Please confirm the postures and their order, the description of the rak'ah, the prayer names and times, the provision for the ill, the note on hand placement and madhhab differences, and the hadith references before publication.

social.hook: In sujud, your highest part, your face, rests on the lowest place, the floor. The Prophet ﷺ taught it is the closest a servant ever comes to Allah. Pride has nowhere to stand.
social.caption: Before the words of the prayer, learn its shape: standing, bowing, and placing your forehead on the ground. Gently, for beginners. Lesson 5 of The New Muslim Path. Buruja.
